herb_parts.name: root
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna root, also known as deadly nightshade, contains tropane alkaloids such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which have been used to treat various conditions including glaucoma and motion sickness. The root has been used in traditional medicine to alleviate pain and reduce inflammation, although its use requires careful dosage and supervision due to its toxicity. In addition, the root has been used to treat respiratory issues like asthma and bronchitis, although its effectiveness and safety for these purposes are still being researched.

herb_parts.name: rhizome
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna rhizome, which is a lesser-known part of the plant, contains tropane alkaloids such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine that have anticholinergic properties. These compounds contribute to the rhizome's potential medicinal uses, including its application in pain relief and as a sedative. However, due to the toxicity of these alkaloids, the rhizome is not commonly used in medicine and its use should be approached with caution.

herb_parts.name: leaf
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna leaf, although not typically used in medicine due to its toxicity, has been used in some traditional remedies for its antispasmodic properties. However, there is limited research on the effectiveness of leaf extracts in treating various health conditions. Some studies suggest that the leaf may contain small amounts of alkaloids, such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which contribute to its medicinal properties.

herb_parts.name: stem
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna stem, a lesser-known part of the plant, contains toxic alkaloids such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which can be used to treat various medical conditions. The stem's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat conditions like glaucoma, as they can reduce intraocular pressure. However, due to their toxicity, the stem's medicinal properties must be carefully extracted and administered under medical supervision.

herb_parts.name: flower
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna flower, although not typically used medicinally due to its toxicity, has been reported to contain small amounts of tropane alkaloids, similar to its other parts. However, its main use lies in the traditional preparation of a homeopathic remedy, often referred to as 'belladonna'. This preparation is said to be used for various purposes, including treating fever, headaches, and skin conditions.

herb_parts.name: fruit
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna fruit, also known as deadly nightshade berries, contain the alkaloids atropine and scopolamine, which are used in medicine to treat various conditions such as bradycardia and to induce dryness of the mouth and eyes. The berries are highly toxic and should be handled with caution, as even small amounts can cause serious harm. In medicinal contexts, the berries are often used in minute amounts, typically in the form of extracts or tinctures, under the guidance of a qualified healthcare professional.

herb_parts.name: seed
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna seed contains a small amount of tropane alkaloids, which have been used in traditional medicine to treat certain conditions. However, it is essential to note that the seeds are not typically used as a medicinal part, and the plant's more significant medicinal value lies in its leaves and berries. The seeds also contain a small amount of solanine, a toxic compound that should be handled with caution.

herb_parts.name: aerial part
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna aerial part contains the alkaloids scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which have been used to treat various conditions, including spasms, bronchitis, and respiratory issues. The aerial part has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of asthma, bronchitis, and other respiratory problems due to its expectorant properties. However, due to its toxicity, the use of Atropa belladonna aerial part must be carefully monitored and guided by a healthcare professional.

herb_parts.name: whole plant
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna whole plant is considered toxic and should be handled with caution, containing tropane alkaloids such as atropine and scopolamine that have medicinal properties. The leaves, roots, and berries of the plant are particularly rich in these alkaloids and are often used to treat various conditions, including muscle spasms and respiratory issues. However, due to the plant's toxicity, it is essential to use it under the guidance of a medical professional.

herb_parts.name: umbel
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna umbel, which is a part of the plant commonly referred to as the flowers or the flowering top, contains tropane alkaloids. These alkaloids, including atropine and scopolamine, have anticholinergic properties, making them useful for various medicinal applications. The anticholinergic properties of the alkaloids in the umbel help to reduce spasms, alleviate pain, and slow down heart rate, among other uses.

herb_parts.name: petiole
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna petiole contains the alkaloids scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which are responsible for the plant's anticholinergic properties. The petiole of Atropa belladonna is also known to have been used in traditional medicine to treat various conditions, including pain and spasms. However, it's worth noting that the petiole of this plant is highly toxic and should only be handled by experienced professionals.

herb_parts.name: bud
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna bud is not typically considered a medicinal part of the plant. However, the seeds of Atropa belladonna contain tropane alkaloids, which are toxic. The berries of the plant are considered the most toxic part and are often associated with poisonings.

herb_parts.name: pith
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna pith contains alkaloids, including scopolamine and hyoscyamine, which have been used in traditional medicine to treat various conditions. However, the pith is primarily known for its high concentration of scopolamine, a tropane alkaloid with anticholinergic properties. The medicinal properties of the pith have been utilized to treat conditions such as anxiety, insomnia, and spasms, although its use is often limited due to its toxicity.

herb_parts.name: bark
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna bark is not a commonly recognized medicinal part, but it has been reported to contain some alkaloids such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine. However, the bark's medicinal properties are not as well-documented or utilized as other parts of the plant, like the leaves and berries.

herb_parts.name: calyx
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna calyx is not a commonly discussed medicinal part of the plant, however, some sources suggest that it may contain small amounts of the tropane alkaloids found in other parts of the plant. The calyx of Atropa belladonna is typically not used for medicinal purposes and is often removed during the harvesting process. There is limited information available on the specific medicinal properties of the calyx, and it is not a recommended source of extracts or compounds.

herb_parts.name: bract
herb_parts.desc: N/A

herb_parts.name: style
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna style, the plant's medicinal properties have been utilized for centuries, with its berries, leaves, and roots being used to treat various ailments. The roots and leaves of Atropa belladonna are considered to be the most potent parts of the plant, containing higher concentrations of alkaloids such as atropine and scopolamine. These alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat conditions like motion sickness, asthma, and even as a pain reliever.

herb_parts.name: stigma
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna stigma is known to have medicinal properties, although it is rare to find references to its use in modern medicine. The stigma of this plant is said to have been used in traditional medicine for its potential antispasmodic and sedative effects. However, due to the plant's toxicity and potential for misuse, its medicinal use is not widely documented or recommended.

herb_parts.name: stamen
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna stamen, like other parts of the plant, contains tropane alkaloids such as scopolamine and atropine, which have been used in medicine for their anticholinergic properties. These compounds can be used to treat various conditions, including bradycardia, asystole, and some types of poisoning. However, due to their toxicity, the use of atropine from Atropa belladonna stamen is highly regulated and typically reserved for emergency situations.

herb_parts.name: petal
herb_parts.desc: Atropa belladonna petal, although not commonly used in traditional medicine, has been reported to contain small amounts of tropane alkaloids, which may contribute to its potential toxicity. However, there is limited scientific evidence to support the use of atropa belladonna petals as a medicinal agent. In general, the toxic nature of atropa belladonna and its potential for misuse makes it inadvisable to use any part of the plant, including the petals, for medicinal purposes.
